step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT moves N+W agent right by 3 cells')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: ** - Scene has 3 panels on gray background, separated by purple vertical bar - Left white panel: charcoal square (door?) + light green 3×3 with white center - Center gap: light green 3×3 with black center + white 3×3 square - Right white panel: charcoal square (door?) - Two N-colored squares likely represent player/agent positions (W=white center, B=black center) - Goal likely involves moving between panels/interacting with doors
- Plan: ** Probe movement to determine which N-square is controllable and how movement works.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed after RIGHT. Let me check what exactly changed by comparing the before and after frames.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after RIGHT
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

before_seg = before.segmentation['nodes']
after_seg = after.segmentation['nodes']

print("BEFORE nodes:")
for n in before_seg:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'W', 'B', 'w', 'c', 'p'):
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} boundary={n['boundary']} children={n['children']}")

print("\nAFTER nodes:")
for n in after_seg:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'W', 'B', 'w', 'c', 'p'):
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} boundary={n['boundary']} children={n['children']}")

# Check prediction result
print("\nPrediction result:", last_prediction_result)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After pressing RIGHT:
1. The left N square (with W center) moved from cols 18-20 to cols 21-23 - it moved right by 3 cells
2. The old position of the left N square became a white 3x3 square (id=7)
3. The old white square (id=8 at cols 30-32) became... wait, let me re-read.
4. The right N square (with B center) stayed at cols 27-29
Wait, let me look more carefully:
- Before: N+W at [30,18]-[32,20], N+B at [30,27]-[32,29], white square at [30,30]-[32,32]
- After: N+W at [30,21]-[32,23], white square at [30,24]-[32,26], N+B at [30,27]-[32,29], white square at [30,30]-[32,32]
So the left N square moved right by 3 columns. Its old position became a white square. The white square that was at [30,30]-[32,32] is still there. But there's also a new white square at [30,24]-[32,26].
Wait, actually it seems like the N+W square moved right by 3 cells, and the white square at [30,30]-[32,32] was originally there. The new white square at [30,24]-[32,26] seems to have appeared.
Actually, looking at the boundaries more carefully:
- Before: white panel boundary included [30, 24], [30, 26], [32, 26], [32, 24] as part of the panel
- After: that area became a separate white square (id=7)
So it seems like the N+W agent moved right by 3 cells, leaving behind a white 3x3 square. The movement seems to be in steps of 3 cells.
Let me also note that the left panel (id=1) boundary changed - it lost the area where the N square was and now has a different shape.
The N+B square didn't move. So the N+W square is the controllable agent.
Let me try more RIGHT to see if I can reach the N+B square or the right panel.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The N+W agent moved right by 3 cells. Let me understand movement better.
# Current agent position: N+W at cols 21-23 (center col 22)
# N+B at cols 27-29 (center col 28)
# White square at cols 24-26 and 30-32
# Purple bar at cols 33-38
# Right panel starts at col 39

# Let me try moving RIGHT again to see if agent moves toward N+B
predict(expect_change=True, note='RIGHT moves N+W agent right by 3 cells')
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
